chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: In the last chapter, you and I started to step through the internal workings of a transformer.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: This is one of the key pieces of technology inside large language models,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: and a lot of other tools in the modern wave of AI. It first hit the scene in a now-famous

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: 2017 paper called Attention is All You Need, and in this chapter, you and I will dig into

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: what this attention mechanism is, visualizing how it processes data.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: As a quick recap, here's the important context I want you to have in mind.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: The goal of the model that you and I are studying is to take in a piece of text and predict what word comes next.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: The input text is broken up into little pieces that we call tokens, and these are very often words or pieces of words,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: but just to make the examples in this video easier for you and me to think about,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: let's simplify by pretending that tokens are always just words.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: The first step in a transformer is to associate each token with a high-dimensional vector, what we call its embedding.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: Now the most important idea I want you to have in mind is how directions in this high-dimensional space of all possible embeddings

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: In the last chapter, we saw an example for how direction can correspond to gender,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: in the sense that adding a certain step in this space can take you from the embedding of a masculine noun

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: to the embedding of the corresponding feminine noun.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: That's just one example you could imagine how many other directions in this high-dimensional space

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: could correspond to numerous other aspects of a word's meaning.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: The aim of a transformer is to progressively adjust these embeddings

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: so that they don't merely encode an individual word,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: but instead they bake in some much, much richer contextual meaning.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: I should say up front that a lot of people find the attention mechanism,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: this key piece in a transformer, very confusing,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: so don't worry if it takes some time for things to sink in.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: I think that before we dive into the computational details and all the matrix multiplications,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: it's worth thinking about a couple examples for the kind of behavior that we want attention to enable.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: Consider the phrases American true mole,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: and take a biopsy of the mole.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: You and I know that the word mole has different meanings in each one of these,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: But after the first step of a transformer,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: the one that breaks up the text and associates each token with a vector,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: the vector that's associated with mole would be the same in all three of these cases,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: because this initial token embedding is effectively a lookup table with no reference to the context.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: It's only in the next step of the transformer

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: that the surrounding embeddings have the chance to pass information into this one.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: The picture you might have in mind is that there are multiple distinct directions in this embedding space,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: encoding the multiple distinct meanings of the word mole,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: and that a well-trained attention block calculates what you need to add to the generic embedding

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: to move it to one of these more specific directions,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: as a function of the context.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: To take another example, consider the embedding of the word tower.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: This is presumably some very generic, non-specific direction in the space,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: associated with lots of other large, tall nouns.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: If this word was immediately preceded by Eiffel,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: you could imagine wanting the mechanism to update this vector

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: so that it points in a direction that more specifically encodes the Eiffel Tower,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: maybe correlated with vectors associated with Paris and France and things made of steel.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: If it was also preceded by the word miniature,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: then the vector should be updated even further

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: so that it no longer correlates with large, tall things.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: More generally than just refining the meaning of a word,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: the attention block allows the model to move information encoded in one embedding

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: potentially ones that are quite far away,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: and potentially with information that's much richer than just a single word.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: What we saw in the last chapter was how,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: after all of the vectors flow through the network,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: the computation that you perform to produce a prediction of the next token

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: is entirely a function of the last vector in the sequence.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: So imagine, for example, that the text you input

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: is most of an entire mystery novel,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: all the way up to a point near the end,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: If the model's going to accurately predict the next word,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: that final vector in the sequence,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: which began its life simply embedding the word was,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: will have to have been updated by all of the attention blocks

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: to represent much, much more than any individual word,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: somehow encoding all of the information

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: that's relevant to predicting the next word.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: To step through the computations, though,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: let's take a much simpler example.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: Imagine that the input includes the phrase,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: A fluffy blue creature roamed the verdant forest.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: suppose that the only type of update that we care about

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: is having the adjectives adjust the meanings of their corresponding nouns.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: What I'm about to describe is what we would call a single head of attention,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: and later we will see how the attention block

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: consists of many different heads run in parallel.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: Again, the initial embedding for each word

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: that only encodes the meaning of that particular word with no context.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: They also encode the position of the word.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: There's a lot more to say about the specific way that positions are encoded,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: but right now, all you need to know is that the entries of this vector

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: are enough to tell you both what the word is

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: and where it exists in the context.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: Let's go ahead and denote these embeddings with the letter E.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: The goal is to have a series of computations

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: produce a new refined set of embeddings

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: where, for example, those corresponding to the nouns

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: have ingested the meaning from their corresponding adjectives.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: And playing the deep learning game,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: we want most of the computations involved

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: to look like matrix vector products

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: where the matrices are full of tunable weights,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: things that the model will learn based on data.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: To be clear, I'm making up this example of adjectives updating nouns

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: just to illustrate the type of behavior

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: that you could imagine an intention had to doing.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: As with so much deep learning,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: the true behavior is much harder to parse

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: because it's based on tweaking and tuning

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: a huge number of parameters to minimize some cost function.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: It's just that as we step through all of the different matrices

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: filled with parameters that are involved in this process,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: I think it's really helpful to have an imagined example

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: of something that it could be doing to help keep it all more concrete.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: For the first step of this process,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: you might imagine each noun, like creature,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: hey, are there any adjectives sitting in front of me?

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: And for the words fluffy and blue,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: to each be able to answer,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: yeah, I'm an adjective and I'm in that position.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: That question is somehow encoded as yet another vector,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: which we call the query for this word.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: has a much smaller dimension than the embedding vector,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: Computing this query looks like taking a certain matrix,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: and multiplying it by the embedding.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: let's write that query vector as Q,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: and then anytime you see me put a matrix

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: next to an arrow like this one,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: multiplying this matrix by the vector at the arrow's start

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: gives you the vector at the arrow's end.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: In this case, you multiply this matrix by all of the embeddings in the context,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: producing one query vector for each token.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: The entries of this matrix are parameters of the model,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: which means the true behavior is learned from data,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: what this matrix does in a particular attention head

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: imagining an example that we might hope that it would learn,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: we'll suppose that this query matrix

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: to certain directions in this smaller query space

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: of looking for adjectives in preceding positions.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: As to what it does to other embeddings,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: Maybe it simultaneously tries to accomplish

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: Right now, we're laser-focused on the nouns.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: associated with this is a second matrix

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: which you also multiply by every one of the embeddings.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: This produces a second sequence of vectors

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: Conceptually, you want to think of the keys

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: This key matrix is also full of tunable parameters,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: and just like the query matrix,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: to that same smaller dimensional space.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: You think of the keys as matching the queries

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: whenever they closely align with each other.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: you would imagine that the key matrix maps the adjectives,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: to vectors that are closely aligned

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: with the query produced by the word creature.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: To measure how well each key matches each query,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: I like to visualize a grid full of a bunch of dots,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: the places where the keys and queries align.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: that would look a little more like this,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: where if the keys produced by fluffy and blue

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: really do align closely with the query produced by creature,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: then the dot products in these two spots

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: would be some large positive numbers.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: that this means the embeddings of fluffy and blue

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: attend to the embedding of creature.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: the dot product between the key for some other word,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: would be some small or negative value

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: that reflects that these are unrelated to each other.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: So we have this grid of values

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: that can be any real number

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: giving us a score for how relevant each word

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: is to updating the meaning of every other word.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: The way we're about to use these scores

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: is to take a certain weighted sum along each column,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: So instead of having values range

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: what we want is for the numbers in these columns

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: And for each column to add up to 1,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: as if they were a probability distribution.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: If you're coming in from the last chapter,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: you know what we need to do then.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: We compute a softmax along each one of these columns

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: after you apply softmax to all of the columns,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: we'll fill in the grid with these normalized values.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: you're safe to think about each column

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: according to how relevant the word on the left

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: is to the corresponding value at the top.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: We call this grid an attention pattern.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: Now if you look at the original transformer paper,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: that they write this all down.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: Here, the variables q and k

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: of query and key vectors respectively,

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: by the query and the key matrices.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: This expression up in the numerator

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: between pairs of keys and queries.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: to divide all of these values

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: by the square root of the dimension

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: that's wrapped around the full expression

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: we'll talk about it in just a second.

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning
chunks.text: Before that, there's one other technical detail

chunks.title: Attention in transformers, step-by-step Deep Learning

text: In the last chapter, you and I started to step through the internal workings of a transformer. This is one of the key pieces of technology inside large language models, and a lot of other tools in the modern wave of AI. It first hit the scene in a now-famous 2017 paper called Attention is All You Need, and in this chapter, you and I will dig into what this attention mechanism is, visualizing how it processes data. As a quick recap, here's the important context I want you to have in mind. The goal of the model that you and I are studying is to take in a piece of text and predict what word comes next. The input text is broken up into little pieces that we call tokens, and these are very often words or pieces of words, but just to make the examples in this video easier for you and me to think about, let's simplify by pretending that tokens are always just words. The first step in a transformer is to associate each token with a high-dimensional vector, what we call its embedding. Now the most important idea I want you to have in mind is how directions in this high-dimensional space of all possible embeddings can correspond with semantic meaning. In the last chapter, we saw an example for how direction can correspond to gender, in the sense that adding a certain step in this space can take you from the embedding of a masculine noun to the embedding of the corresponding feminine noun. That's just one example you could imagine how many other directions in this high-dimensional space could correspond to numerous other aspects of a word's meaning. The aim of a transformer is to progressively adjust these embeddings so that they don't merely encode an individual word, but instead they bake in some much, much richer contextual meaning. I should say up front that a lot of people find the attention mechanism, this key piece in a transformer, very confusing, so don't worry if it takes some time for things to sink in. I think that before we dive into the computational details and all the matrix multiplications, it's worth thinking about a couple examples for the kind of behavior that we want attention to enable. Consider the phrases American true mole, one mole of carbon dioxide, and take a biopsy of the mole. You and I know that the word mole has different meanings in each one of these, based on the context. But after the first step of a transformer, the one that breaks up the text and associates each token with a vector, the vector that's associated with mole would be the same in all three of these cases, because this initial token embedding is effectively a lookup table with no reference to the context. It's only in the next step of the transformer that the surrounding embeddings have the chance to pass information into this one. The picture you might have in mind is that there are multiple distinct directions in this embedding space, encoding the multiple distinct meanings of the word mole, and that a well-trained attention block calculates what you need to add to the generic embedding to move it to one of these more specific directions, as a function of the context. To take another example, consider the embedding of the word tower. This is presumably some very generic, non-specific direction in the space, associated with lots of other large, tall nouns. If this word was immediately preceded by Eiffel, you could imagine wanting the mechanism to update this vector so that it points in a direction that more specifically encodes the Eiffel Tower, maybe correlated with vectors associated with Paris and France and things made of steel. If it was also preceded by the word miniature, then the vector should be updated even further so that it no longer correlates with large, tall things. More generally than just refining the meaning of a word, the attention block allows the model to move information encoded in one embedding to that of another, potentially ones that are quite far away, and potentially with information that's much richer than just a single word. What we saw in the last chapter was how, after all of the vectors flow through the network, including many different attention blocks, the computation that you perform to produce a prediction of the next token is entirely a function of the last vector in the sequence. So imagine, for example, that the text you input is most of an entire mystery novel, all the way up to a point near the end, which reads, therefore the murderer was. If the model's going to accurately predict the next word, that final vector in the sequence, which began its life simply embedding the word was, will have to have been updated by all of the attention blocks to represent much, much more than any individual word, somehow encoding all of the information from the full context window that's relevant to predicting the next word. To step through the computations, though, let's take a much simpler example. Imagine that the input includes the phrase, A fluffy blue creature roamed the verdant forest. And for the moment, suppose that the only type of update that we care about is having the adjectives adjust the meanings of their corresponding nouns. What I'm about to describe is what we would call a single head of attention, and later we will see how the attention block consists of many different heads run in parallel. Again, the initial embedding for each word is some high-dimensional vector that only encodes the meaning of that particular word with no context. Actually, that's not quite true. They also encode the position of the word. There's a lot more to say about the specific way that positions are encoded, but right now, all you need to know is that the entries of this vector are enough to tell you both what the word is and where it exists in the context. Let's go ahead and denote these embeddings with the letter E. The goal is to have a series of computations produce a new refined set of embeddings where, for example, those corresponding to the nouns have ingested the meaning from their corresponding adjectives. And playing the deep learning game, we want most of the computations involved to look like matrix vector products where the matrices are full of tunable weights, things that the model will learn based on data. To be clear, I'm making up this example of adjectives updating nouns just to illustrate the type of behavior that you could imagine an intention had to doing. As with so much deep learning, the true behavior is much harder to parse because it's based on tweaking and tuning a huge number of parameters to minimize some cost function. It's just that as we step through all of the different matrices filled with parameters that are involved in this process, I think it's really helpful to have an imagined example of something that it could be doing to help keep it all more concrete. For the first step of this process, you might imagine each noun, like creature, asking the question, hey, are there any adjectives sitting in front of me? And for the words fluffy and blue, to each be able to answer, yeah, I'm an adjective and I'm in that position. That question is somehow encoded as yet another vector, another list of numbers, which we call the query for this word. This query vector, though, has a much smaller dimension than the embedding vector, say 128. Computing this query looks like taking a certain matrix, which I'll label WQ, and multiplying it by the embedding. Compressing things a bit, let's write that query vector as Q, and then anytime you see me put a matrix next to an arrow like this one, it's meant to represent that multiplying this matrix by the vector at the arrow's start gives you the vector at the arrow's end. In this case, you multiply this matrix by all of the embeddings in the context, producing one query vector for each token. The entries of this matrix are parameters of the model, which means the true behavior is learned from data, and in practice, what this matrix does in a particular attention head is challenging to parse. But for our sake, imagining an example that we might hope that it would learn, we'll suppose that this query matrix maps the embeddings of nouns to certain directions in this smaller query space that somehow encodes the notion of looking for adjectives in preceding positions. As to what it does to other embeddings, who knows? Maybe it simultaneously tries to accomplish some other goal with those. Right now, we're laser-focused on the nouns. At the same time, associated with this is a second matrix called the key matrix, which you also multiply by every one of the embeddings. This produces a second sequence of vectors that we call the keys. Conceptually, you want to think of the keys as potentially answering the queries. This key matrix is also full of tunable parameters, and just like the query matrix, it maps the embedding vectors to that same smaller dimensional space. You think of the keys as matching the queries whenever they closely align with each other. In our example, you would imagine that the key matrix maps the adjectives, like fluffy and blue, to vectors that are closely aligned with the query produced by the word creature. To measure how well each key matches each query, you compute a dot product between each possible key-query pair. I like to visualize a grid full of a bunch of dots, where the bigger dots correspond to the larger dot products, the places where the keys and queries align. For our adjective noun example, that would look a little more like this, where if the keys produced by fluffy and blue really do align closely with the query produced by creature, then the dot products in these two spots would be some large positive numbers. In the lingo, machine learning people would say that this means the embeddings of fluffy and blue attend to the embedding of creature. By contrast, the dot product between the key for some other word, like the, and the query for creature, would be some small or negative value that reflects that these are unrelated to each other. So we have this grid of values that can be any real number from negative infinity to infinity, giving us a score for how relevant each word is to updating the meaning of every other word. The way we're about to use these scores is to take a certain weighted sum along each column, weighted by the relevance. So instead of having values range from negative infinity to infinity, what we want is for the numbers in these columns to be between 0 and 1. And for each column to add up to 1, as if they were a probability distribution. If you're coming in from the last chapter, you know what we need to do then. We compute a softmax along each one of these columns to normalize the values. In our picture, after you apply softmax to all of the columns, we'll fill in the grid with these normalized values. At this point, you're safe to think about each column as giving weights according to how relevant the word on the left is to the corresponding value at the top. We call this grid an attention pattern. Now if you look at the original transformer paper, there's a really compact way that they write this all down. Here, the variables q and k represent the full arrays of query and key vectors respectively, those little vectors you get by multiplying the embeddings by the query and the key matrices. This expression up in the numerator is a really compact way to represent the grid of all possible dot products between pairs of keys and queries. A small technical detail that I didn't mention is that for numerical stability, it happens to be helpful to divide all of these values by the square root of the dimension in that key query space. Then this softmax that's wrapped around the full expression is meant to be understood to apply column by column. As to that v term, we'll talk about it in just a second. Before that, there's one other technical detail that so far I've skipped. During the training process, when you run this model on a given text example, and all of the weights are slightly adjusted and tuned to either reward or punish it based on how high a probability it assigns to the true next word in the passage, it turns out to make the whole training process a lot more efficient if you simultaneously have it predict every possible next token following each initial sub-sequence of tokens in this passage. For example, with the phrase that we've been focusing on, it might also be predicting what words follow creature and what words follow the. This is really nice because it means what would otherwise be a single training example effectively acts as many. For the purposes of our attention pattern, it means that you never want to allow later words to influence earlier words since otherwise they could kind of give away the answer for what comes next. What this means is that we want all of these spots here, the ones representing later tokens influencing earlier ones, to somehow be forced to be zero. The simplest thing you might think to do is to set them equal to zero, but if you did that, the columns wouldn't add up to one anymore. They wouldn't be normalized. So instead, a common way to do this is that before applying softmax, you set all of those entries to be negative infinity. If you do that, then after applying softmax, all of those get turned into zero, but the columns stay normalized. This process is called masking. There are versions of attention where you don't apply it, but in our GPT example, even though this is more relevant during the training phase than it would be, say, running it as a chatbot or something like that, you do always apply this masking to prevent later tokens from influencing earlier ones. Another fact that's worth reflecting on about this attention pattern is how its size is equal to the square of the context size. So this is why context size can be a really huge bottleneck for large language models, and scaling it up is non-trivial. As you might imagine, motivated by a desire for bigger and bigger context windows, recent years have seen some variations to the attention mechanism aimed at making context more scalable, but right here, you and I are staying focused on the basics. Okay, great. Computing this pattern lets the model deduce which words are relevant to which other words. Now, you need to actually update the embeddings, allowing words to pass information to whichever other words they're relevant to. For example, you want the embedding of fluffy to somehow cause a change to creature that moves it to a different part of this 12,000-dimensional embedding space that more specifically encodes a fluffy creature. What I'm going to do here is first show you the most straightforward way that you could do this, though there's a slight way that this gets modified in the context of multi-headed attention. This most straightforward way would be to use a third matrix, what we call the value matrix, which you multiply by the embedding of that first word, for example, fluffy. The result of this is what you would call a value vector, and this is something that you add to the embedding of the second word. In this case, something you add to the embedding of creature. So this value vector lives in the same very high-dimensional space as the embeddings. When you multiply this value matrix by the embedding of a word, you might think of it as saying, if this word is relevant to adjusting the meaning of something else, what exactly should be added to the embedding of that something else in order to reflect this? Looking back in our diagram, let's set aside all of the keys and the queries, since after you compute the attention pattern, you're done with those. Then you're going to take this value matrix and multiply it by every one of those embeddings to produce a sequence of value vectors. You might think of these value vectors as being kind of associated with the corresponding keys. For each column in this diagram, you multiply each of the value vectors by the corresponding weight in that column. For example, here, under the embedding of creature, you would be adding large proportions of the value vectors for fluffy and blue, while all of the other value vectors get zeroed out, or at least nearly zeroed out. And then finally, the way to actually update the embedding associated with this column, previously encoding some context-free meaning of creature, you add together all of these rescaled values in the column, producing a change that you want to add, that I'll label delta E, and then you add that to the original embedding. Hopefully, what results is a more refined vector encoding the more contextually rich meaning, like that of a fluffy blue creature. And, of course, you don't just do this to one embedding, you apply the same weighted sum across all of the columns in this picture, producing a sequence of changes. Adding all of those changes to the corresponding embeddings produces a full sequence of more refined embeddings popping out of the attention block. Zooming out, this whole process is what you would describe as a single head of attention. As I've described things so far, this process is parametrized by three distinct matrices, all filled with tunable parameters, the key, the query, and the value. I want to take a moment to continue what we started in the last chapter with the scorekeeping where we count up the total number of model parameters using the numbers from GPT-3. These key and query matrices each have 12,288 columns, matching the embedding dimension, and 128 rows, matching the dimension of that smaller key query space. This gives us an additional 1.5 million or so parameters for each one. If you look at that value matrix by contrast, the way I've described things so far would suggest that it's a square matrix that has 12,288 columns and 12,288 rows, since both its inputs and its outputs live in this very large embedding space. If true, that would mean about 150 million added parameters. And to be clear, you could do that. You could devote orders of magnitude more parameters to the value map than to the key in query.
